metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_1615
metadata.comment:
Rationale: The authors identify syt-9 (previously reported as syt-V) as functional Ca2+ sensor in evoked NT release in rescue experiments carried out in a syt-1 KO background. Xu et al infect syt-1 KO neurons with various syts and observe rescue of fast synchrounous neuronal transmission (IPSCs and EPSCs evoked by single action potentials (APs)) only upon expression of syt-1, syt-2 or syt-9. Interestingly, the authors demonstrate that all three syt isoforms rescue not only the amount of synaptic transmission (measured as synaptic charge transfer, Fig. 1B-C), but also the fast kinetics of release (Fig. 1D). Furthermore, the rescue with syt-9 reverses all effects from syt-1 KO such as loss of initial phase of release during high Hz stimulus, changed paired-pulse ratio between the first two stimuli and ratio of delayed release to the first response (Fig. 2 B, C, D, H) without any effect on asynchronous release.
In order to determine the effect of syt-9 deletion on Ca2+-triggered exocytosis, the authors generated conditional knock-in mice: Cultured striatal neurons obtained from these mice were infected with lentiviral constructs encoding either WT or inactive Cre-recombinase in order to obtain a test and control neuronal culture, respectively. By comparison of IPSCs evoked by single APs in WT and KI neurons, Xu et al show that KI itself does not perturb synaptic transmission. When the rescue construct is not induced, a severe decrease in synaptic amplitude and synaptic charge transfer of evoked IPSCs is observed (Fig. 7A and B). The authors exclude effect on synaptic transmission from altered size of RRP (in KI and WT striatal neurons) as recordings after hypertonic sucrose treatment reveal no differences. Based on these findings, Xu et al suggest that syt-9 deletion results specifically in impairment of Ca2+-triggered release (decreased release probability causes decrease in fast synchronous release).. Experimental description: Materials and Methods (Neuronal Cultures and Lentiviral Infection
Primary striatal neurons were isolated from newborn homozygous synaptotagmin-
9 KI mice, dissociated by trysin digestion, and plated on
Poly-D-lysine-coated glass coverslips
